learning_outcomes.description: Appreciate the complex, contested and political nature of peacebuilding.
Understand the cultural foundations of dominant and innovative/ alternative approaches to political ordering for peacebuilding.

Demonstrate responsible use of generative AI to support peacebuilding research and analysis, while recognising the importance of human judgement and verification, contextual knowledge, and ethical considerations.

Collaborate effectively in teams to evaluate competing peacebuilding priorities, facilitate collective decisions, and communicate coherent and evidence-informed recommendations for preventing violent conflict and sustaining peace.
Explain key peacebuilding concepts and apply peacebuilding knowledge to ethical and social dilemmas in conflict-affected societies.

assessments.task_description:
Reading the required texts in the course's reading list is essential for coming prepared to class and for success in POLS3511.

In each seminar in Weeks 2-8 and Weeks 10-11, you will be given 10 minutes to complete a short, handwritten, closed book quiz. The precise timing of the quiz during the seminar's 3-hour window may vary week-by-week.

Each quiz is worth 5 marks, divided in two parts. The first part will be two multiple choice questions based on the required readings, worth 1 point each. The second part of the quiz is short answer, and will ask you to define a key concept of one of the readings (worth 1 pt) and explain how this concept helps to understand or critically assess peacebuilding practice, based on the key points the author(s) made in the reading (worth a maximum 2 pts).

While there are 9 opportunities to take the quiz, only 6 will count towards your final mark, up to a maximum of 30%. Your top 6 results will comprise your mark if you take more than 6 quizzes. Since this allows you to miss up to three quizzes, you will not be able to re-sit individual quizzes and no alternative assessment will be available. 

If you have a disability plan that prevents you from taking hand-written quizzes, please get in touch with the course's staff as soon as possible to find suitable arrangements. Special consideration for alternative assessment will only be given under highly unusual and well-documented circumstances, to students who are unable to attend more than three seminars during which quizzes are taking place. As there'll be only one opportunity to take an alternative quiz please let the course coordinator know no later than 6 October and provide all the necessary documentation.

Please note:

This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempt to use AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will be given 10 minutes to complete a hand-written, closed book quiz. Quizzes will be completed in-class and submitted immediately to the lecturer.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Peacebuilding Investment Board: Group Report (4000 words)
assessments.task_description:
Each student will be assigned a group, who comprise the members of the Board of the Global Peacebuilding Fund (GPF), an international funding mechanism established to prevent the outbreak, escalation, continuation, and recurrence of violent conflict and support sustainable peace.

GPF has US$1 billion available to invest over the next five years. Demand for funding significantly exceeds available resources, meaning difficult choices must be made about what to fund, where to invest, and which intiatives are most likely to contribute to sustainable peace.

Your group’s task is to develop a 4000-word peacebuilding investment strategy report. The report should :

1 Begin with Executive Summary and brief overview of the strategic rationale guiding your funding decisions:

Often when UN agencies, bilateral donors, and development banks decide where to channel funding they offer an overarching justification for why the activities are strategically aligned with the context, evidence, and intended outcomes. Your group is tasked with doing the same: to offer a strategic rationale for your funding decisions. A strategic rationale is an explanation and justification for why your group has chosen to invest in particular initiatives as the most appropriate and effective way to achieve the desired objective of sustainable peace. For example, should funding be prioritised based on:

  • Greatest conflict risk(s)? On what basis is risk determined?
  • Value for money: addressing ongoing conflicts or preventing future conflict?
  • Political opportunity structure: political constraints/opportunities?
  • Legitimacy or positionality of actor: UN-led efforts, state building/governance initiatives, or local/community peacebuilding?
  • A particular region or conflict context?
  • A particular type of initiative? governance, mediation and peace processes, climate adapatation, security, transitional justice, social cohesion, gender and youth, etc.
  • Addressing the root causes of conflict? How are these assessed?
  • Diversified funding portfolio or catalytic initiative (e.g., like the Rotary Polio Eradication Initiative)

There is no single correct approach. However, your funding decisions should reflect a clear, coherent, and well-justified rationale for how your investment portfolio is going to advance sustainable peace.

2 Clearly identify what you are funding:

  • Identify the institutions, initiatives, programs, or sectors receiving funding; the amount allocated to each; and any geographic or regional priorities in the funding allocation. Your peace investment portfolio should account for the full US$1billion. [Note: this may seem like a lot of money, but by comparison Australia’s ODA budget is ~$5.2bn for the 2026-27 financial year; before it was axed, USAID oversaw a budget of approximately $35bn/pa—so you have tough decisions to make!]

3 Justify your funding decisions:

  • Explain and justify why these are good investments in peace based on: the best available evidence of what works (or does not work) in peacebuilding and conflict prevention; conflict data and analysis; cultural and ethical considerations; political feasibility; likelihood of impact; potential risks and limitations.

4 Identify how used AI:

  • You are required to use generative AI tools for the purposes of synthesising the corpus of data, reports, and policy documents that you have acquired for your research. You are expected to select a significant and relevant body of information that would otherwise be unfeasible to analyse in a short period of time.
  • You may use AI for other technical elements of the report (e.g. structuring), however you are required to include an annex to the report that provides an overview of how AI was used: What tasks did AI assist with? What AI platform did you use? What, if any, sources did you upload? Provide 3-5 examples of prompts that significantly influenced your analysis or funding decisions, and explain how they shaped your thinking. How did you verify AI-generated information? What were the limitations, biases, or weaknesses? Were there AI-generated recommendations that you rejected, why?

You must strictly abide by copyright rules—meaning no copyrighted material should be uploaded into an AI platform. Groups will not be assessed on the extent to which they used AI or on their technological proficiency, but on their ability to use AI critically, transparently, and responsibly. Demonstrating awareness of AI’s limitations is just as important as demonstrating its utility.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Your task is to deliver a 10-minute powerpoint presentation to the class on your funding strategy report, covering its key components. This will be followed by an 8-minute Q&A in which each group member will be asked to respond to a question by the course teaching team. Presentations will be recorded.

This assignment is worth 20 marks: 10 are based on the quality of the group presentation; 10 are based the quality of your individual response.  The questions will be based on the prompts from the written assignment (e.g., asking you to clarify or justify your strategic rationale, funding decisions, explain evidence and resources used, etc). Your group may also be asked to respond to questions from instructors and classmates about your use of AI.

You should upload your powerpoint presentation to TurnitIn by 5pm on the day of your presentation.
assessments.submission_guidelines: In addition to your in-class group presentation, you must submit your powerpoint presentation electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin, by 5pm on the day of your presentation.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
A critical reflection is a piece of writing which requires you to think about your experiences, challenges and successes, and create a plan of improvement for the future. Coherent thought and deliberate planning based on probable outcomes create the strongest critical reflections (as opposed to stream of consciousness and guesswork).

Your task is to write a 2500-word critical reflection essay. It should:

1.    Critically reflect on your funding strategy and process:

  • Identify trade-offs you made: Because resources are limited, funding one initiative means not funding another. What trade-offs did your group face?; What initiatives or approaches did you choose not to fund, why?
  • How did evidence drive your decision-making? What counted as reliable evidence?
  • How did you work together as a group?: What was your most contested collective decision? How did you resolve it? What is one piece of advice you would give for more effective team work, based on your experience? 

2.    Drawing on specific examples from other group presentations, critically reflect on how your funding strategy and decisions compared to other groups. Consider questions such as:

  • What different strategic rationales emerged across the class? Were some approaches more persuasive than others?
  • What types of evidence were most effectively used? What evidence seemed the strongest versus most contested/uncertain?
  • Did certain assumptions about peacebuilding appear to dominate decision-making? What were they?
  • In retrospect, and based on what you learned from other group presentations, would you make different funding decisions? Why or why not?
  • Reflect on the use of AI, both within your group and across the class. Your discussion should consider: whether AI performed well (or poorly) at particular tasks; any common errors, omissions, or biases that became apparent; examples where AI may have influenced funding decisions in problematic ways; whether/how policymakers should use AI in making complex peacebuilding decisions.

3.    Draw on literature and debates we covered in the course to critically reflect on whether important issues were marginalised, overlooked, or undervalued in the funding exercise, or on whether specific efforts were made to meaningfully address these issues.

  •  Consider issues such as: culture; inclusion; local ownership; relational peace and Indigenous approaches; long-term versus short-term imperatives.

4.    Reflecting on limitations in this funding exercise, propose one new directive/reform for the Global Peacebuilding Fund Board assignment that could improve future group’s decision-making processes, inclusivity, or effectiveness. Explain the problem your reform addresses and how it would improve future funding decisions.

Please note:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
